coredata.dc:title: Induced toxicity in early-life stage zebrafish (Danio rerio) and its behavioral analysis after exposure to non-doped, nitrogen-doped and nitrogen, sulfur-co doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chousidis, Ieremias
Stalikas, Constantine D.
Leonardos, Ioannis D.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the effects of doping of CQDs with alternative functional groups (dopants) were evaluated through embryonic development of zebrafish (Danio rerio). The CQDs were synthesized using simple and low-cost sources: Non-doped (citric acid was used as the carbon source), nitrogen-doped (N-doped) and nitrogen, sulfur-co-doped (N,S-doped). The CQDs induced significant toxicity to zebrafish (>150 μg/mL) and the toxic effects were dose-dependent. The N,S-doped CQDs were the most toxic (LD50 = 149.92 μg/mL), followed by the N-doped CQDs (LD50 = 399.95 μg/mL) while the non-doped CQDs were the least toxic (LD50 = 548.48 μg/mL) of the three. The growth rate (GR) was affected following the toxicity pattern (GRNS-doped<GRN-doped<GRnon-doped <GRblanc), which, in turn, greatly depends on the type of dopant. Morphological malformations, such as pericardial edema, yolk sac edema, tail and spinal curvature were observed to zebrafish embryos as the toxicity, concentration and exposure time to the nanomaterial increased. Behavioral analysis showed that locomotor activity increases as the toxicity of the nanomaterial rises. The differences in toxicity, growth rate and malfunctions of CQDs were attributed to their doping with different heteroatoms. The N,S-doped CQDs, unequivocally, exhibited the most pronounced effects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Zebrafish
Danio rerio
Nanomaterials
Zebrafish embryo toxicity test

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of A. non-doped, B. N-doped and C. N,S co-doped CQDs. Scale: 20 nm.
Mortality curves of zebrafish larvae exposed to the three different CQDs.

Malformations of zebrafish embryos exposed to CQDs. (A-D) Control larvae, (E-H) larvae exposed to 150, 250, 300, 600 μg/mL respectively to non-doped CQDs with indicative pericardial edema and tail bending, (I-L) larvae exposed to 150, 250, 300, 600 μg/mL respectively to N-doped CQDs with indicative pericardial edema and tail bending, (M-P) larvae exposed to 75, 100, 300, 400 μg/mL respectively to N,S-doped CQDs with indicative pericardial edema and tail bending, (Q-T) larvae exposed to 75, 100, 300, 400 μg/mL respectively to N,S-doped CQDs with indicative pericardial edema and tail bending. Malformations are indicated by arrows.
Length variation for larvae exposed to non-doped, N-doped and N,S-doped CQDs with respect to concentration and time.
Effect of non-doped, N-doped, and N,S-doped CQDs on the total distance (mm) of zebrafish larvae after a 30-min light-to-dark photostimulation, at 144 hpf.

head.ce:title: Induced toxicity in early-life stage zebrafish (Danio rerio) and its behavioral analysis after exposure to non-doped, nitrogen-doped and nitrogen, sulfur-co doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Zoology, Biological Applications and Technology Department, University of Ioannina, 45110, Greece
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Zoology, Biological Applications and Technology Department, University of Ioannina, 45110 Greece

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110, Greece
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110 Greece

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Laboratory of Zoology, Biological Applications and Technology Department, University of Ioannina, 45110, Greece.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the effects of doping of CQDs with alternative functional groups (dopants) were evaluated through embryonic development of zebrafish (Danio rerio). The CQDs were synthesized using simple and low-cost sources: Non-doped (citric acid was used as the carbon source), nitrogen-doped (N-doped) and nitrogen, sulfur-co-doped (N,S-doped). The CQDs induced significant toxicity to zebrafish (>150 μg/mL) and the toxic effects were dose-dependent. The N,S-doped CQDs were the most toxic (LD50 = 149.92 μg/mL), followed by the N-doped CQDs (LD50 = 399.95 μg/mL) while the non-doped CQDs were the least toxic (LD50 = 548.48 μg/mL) of the three. The growth rate (GR) was affected following the toxicity pattern (GRNS-doped<GRN-doped<GRnon-doped <GRblanc), which, in turn, greatly depends on the type of dopant. Morphological malformations, such as pericardial edema, yolk sac edema, tail and spinal curvature were observed to zebrafish embryos as the toxicity, concentration and exposure time to the nanomaterial increased. Behavioral analysis showed that locomotor activity increases as the toxicity of the nanomaterial rises. The differences in toxicity, growth rate and malfunctions of CQDs were attributed to their doping with different heteroatoms. The N,S-doped CQDs, unequivocally, exhibited the most pronounced effects.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are a class of materials with unique properties and promising characteristics (Zhang et al., 2016), offering several applications in the fields of medicine (bioimaging, drug delivery), technology (biosensing) and environmental sciences (energy conversion and energy storage) (Lim et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2009). Their nanoscopic scale (less than 100 nm in size) (Wang and Hu, 2014) and the important optical, electrical and conductive properties (sensing, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, etc.) (Zheng et al., 2015) are some of their main characteristics.

In the most recent years, a specific class of nanomaterials, the so-called carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have drawn intensive attention and triggered substantial investigation. They consist of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur and other common elements (Wang and Hu, 2014) and manifest superior merits including excellent biocompatibility both in vitro and in vivo, resistance to photobleaching, easy surface functionalization and bio-conjugation, outstanding colloidal stability, eco-friendly synthesis and low cost. All these properties endow them with the high potential of replacing the conventional fluorescent heavy metal-containing semiconductor quantum dots or organic dyes (Osborne, 2013). The wide range of applications and the benefits offered by certain classes of nanomaterials have raised concerns about their potential toxic effects on organisms. For instance, metal-based quantum dots (Ag, Cd, Ti quantum dots) due to their minuscule size and low stability tend to interact with other substances, thereby causing toxic effects (Osborne, 2013; Chatzimitakos et al., 2016). Studies have shown that heavy metal-based quantum dots cause oxidative stress to fish brain cells (Oberdörster, 2004), DNA damage to mice exposed to nanoparticles (Trouiller et al., 2009) and high lethality rate in invertebrates, such as Daphnia magna, at concentrations in the range of 5.5 mg/L to 143 mg/L (Lovern et al., 2007; Zhu et al., 2009). For these reasons, researchers turned their interest to alternatives, which are more eco-friendly and less toxic. More importantly, compared with traditional metallic quantum dots, CQDs are thought to be environmentally benign and safer for biological uses as most of them exhibit quite lower toxicity in cell lines and mice (Lim et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2009; Kang et al., 2015). An important issue when trying to scientifically approach CQDs is that due to their minuscule size they tend to interact with other chemical/biological factors and their mechanism of action remains completely unknown or obscure (Lim et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2009). Specifically, CQDs may potentially cause toxic responses to aquatic ecosystems, which act as a sink for most contaminants (Osborne, 2013) and aquatic organisms and are considered to be the final recipients (Daughton, 2004; Valavanidis and Vlachogianni, 2010). Most of the studies confirmed that no noticeable signs of toxicity of CQDs have been observed in in vitro cells and in vivo animal models (Yang et al., 2009; Kang et al., 2015).

Zebrafish is a vertebrate animal model suitable for ecotoxicological, drug and chemical toxicity studies because of its high growth rate, especially at early life stages, of the small size of its larvae (about 4 mm), easy and low-cost maintenance, short generation time (Lin et al., 2016) and of the ability to lay a large number of transparent eggs (approximately 300 eggs) every 2–3 days (Wixon, 2000). Zebrafish is, conceivably, a "high-throughput" model system for various screening tests (i.e. toxicological, pharmacological, etc.) as a great number of small molecules may, in fact, permeate the chorion (Kokel et al., 2010). Moreover, it is highly homologous to other mammals (Kang et al., 2015; Rihel, 2010) and it has been shown to share many similarities in bioprocesses and genome with humans (Spitsbergen and Kent, 2007). In addition, zebrafish is used as an experimental model organism in behavioral studies due to the high development rate and the capability to study its locomotor activity (Rihel, 2010; Wang et al., 2018; Pitt et al., 2018). Lastly, studies employing the zebrafish as a model organism, whereby major ethical issues with animal laboratory use can be overcome, have greatly increased in the past years (Wiecinski, 2012).

As the use of nanomaterials in both commercial goods and novel applications expands, the need for relevant, accurate and predictive nanotoxicological assessment also arises. Moreover, the impact of CQDs on the environment is foreseen to grow as their burden will increase, because of their intensive study with vast potential scope in various fields even for high-volume applications. In this study, the potential acute and developmental toxicity of three CQDs of different elemental composition is investigated on zebrafish larvae with the aim to examine the potential risks on aquatic organisms and the environment and to draw intensive attention and trigger further investigation. The toxicity assays focus on testing the toxic responses induced in several levels of an organism (acute toxicity tests, morphological malformations, behavioral analysis tests). Non-doped and doped with nitrogen and sulfur-nitrogen were the three main CQD variants. The studied nanomaterials are well known for their multiple properties and applications and share in common the carbon source deriving from citric acid.

The CQDs were synthesized using citric acid as a carbon source, based on the procedures described elsewhere (Yongqiang et al., 2013; Soumalya et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2015). Details about the procedures are given in the Supporting Information.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements to identify the functionalities of the nanomaterials were obtained on a Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two FT-IR (Perkin Elmer Inc., MA, USA) using an attenuated total reflectance accessory. UV–vis spectra were obtained using a Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Germany) and fluorescence measurements were conducted on an RF 5301 PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan), with a quartz cell of 1 × 1 cm. High resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope operated at 200 kV equipped with LaB6 filament. Each sample was prepared by depositing a drop of an aqueous CQDs dispersion on carbon-coated copper grids and drying, at room temperature.

Adult zebrafish of the wild-type strain (AB) were raised and maintained at 28 ± 1 °C with a 14 h light/10 h dark photoperiod (lights on at 8:00 a.m.) in a recirculating system (Tecniplast Zebtec, Buguggiate, Italy). The water supplied to the system was filtered through reverse osmosis, with pH 6.5–7.5 and conductivity to 500 ± 50 μS/cm. Zebrafish were fed twice daily with aquaculture zebrafish feed. Sexually mature zebrafish (at least three-months old) were used for spawning. Two females and one male were placed in every single breeding tank in the afternoon, a day before reproduction took place and were separated with a net. Next morning, the net was removed before the onset of light and the mating lasted 2 or 3 h. The eggs were collected after completion of mating and pooled in zebrafish E3 embryo medium (5 mmol/L NaCl, 0.33 mmol/L CaCl2, 0.33 mmol/L MgSO4·7H2O and 0.17 mmol/L KCl). The zebrafish use and handling protocol conformed to the Guidelines on the protection of experimental animals by the Council of Europe (European Directive 2010/63, 2020European Directive 2010/63, 2020), which allows zebrafish embryos to be used up to the moment of free-living (approximately 5–7 days after fertilization). As the embryos used here were not more than 5-days old, no license was required according to the Council of Europe (European Directive 2010/63, 2020European Directive 2010/63, 2020; OECD, 2013), Directive 86/ 609/EEC. The embryos were examined at 4 h post-fertilization (hpf), under a dissecting light microscope and stereoscope (Olympus BX43, Olympus KL300 stereoscope). The eggs were inspected and the unfertilized, as well as those that showed obvious malformations were removed and the remaining, were selected for further experiments.

Fertilized zebrafish eggs were collected in petri dishes containing E3 embryo medium. After 24 h, the embryos were dechorionated (OECD, 2013) and placed into 24-well culture plates (2 embryos per well, 1.5 mL of solution per well). The chorion, which acts as a protective barrier, was removed in order to increase the sensitivity of the testing and to successfully test the effects of any substance according to OECD (2013). For each nanomaterial, five different concentrations were tested based on their toxicity data, after performing preliminary tests. For non-doped, N-doped and N,S-doped CQDs, the tested concentration ranges were 150−1200 μg/mL, 250−1000 μg/mL and 75−400 μg/mL, respectively. After synthesis, the CQDs were characterized and used in the preparation of stock CQD solutions in E3 buffer, which was employed as control, based on the European Guideline for Fish Embryo Acute Toxicity (FET).

The duration of each experiment was set at 96 h (OECD, 2013), beginning at approximately 24 hpf, at 26-somite point, according to Kimmel (Kimmel et al., 1995). The developmental status of the zebrafish larvae was recorded every 24 h. The survival rate (%) was determined from the remaining living larvae. Larvae were considered dead if any of the following conditions were recorded: 1) coagulation of the embryo, 2) non-detachment of the tail, 3) lack of heartbeat and 4) severe pericardial edema with lack of heartbeat (OECD, 2013). Embryonic malformations were observed using a stereoscope (Olympus BX43) equipped with a digital camera and high-resolution pictures were taken and processed with the Image-Pro Plus software (Image-Pro Plus 7, Media Cybernetics).

The testing CQD solutions were renewed daily and the results were considered valid when the survival rate in the control was 90 %. After 96 h of exposure, the embryos were transferred to clean water after several rinses with E3 medium embryo buffer in order to remove the residues of CQDs that have been attached to the larvae signifying the end of the exposure of zebrafish to CQDs and then we proceeded with further analysis. The toxicity assay included recording of the malformations and calculation of LD50 after exposure to each nanomaterial. The entire experiment was repeated three times in order to validate the results and assess their precision.

After the end of the exposure period (96 hpf), larvae were transferred to an isolated behavioral screening room in small containers (500 mL) with E3 embryo medium and were fed with commercial feed. At 144 hpf larvae were kept in the dark at 28 °C for 1 h before video tracking and then, they were placed in 24-well plates. A single larva with 1.5 mL of E3 embryo medium was placed in each well (Ahmad et al., 2012; Noyes et al., 2015). Plates were transferred into a behavioral testing chamber, which was equipped with a temperature control unit (DanioVision, Noldus Inc., Wageningen, The Netherlands). The larvae were left for 10 min for acclimatization and then, the video tracking cycle was set as follows: 10 min dark period, 10 min light and 10 min dark (Ahmad et al., 2012; Noyes et al., 2015; Tzima et al., 2017; Crosby et al., 2015). This tracking cycle was conducted in order to analyze the response of larvae to external stimuli, such as light and dark cycles following normal conditions (Pitt et al., 2018; Tzima et al., 2017; Crosby et al., 2015; Sledge et al., 2011). Video recordings were held between 10 a.m. and 12 p.m. in order to stabilize some basal activities related to circadian rhythms (Eddins et al., 2009; Hirayama et al., 2005; Ben-Moshe et al., 2014). Tracks were analyzed for the distance moved (cm) and velocity (cm/s) in relation to light/dark periods. The behavioral alterations of zebrafish were compared with those of the control sample. The behavioral analysis was performed with the aid of Ethovision XT 14 software (DanioVision, Noldus Inc., Wageningen, The Netherlands).

The toxicity assays (LD50 calculation) and confidence intervals were determined using the probit analysis. The effect and correlation between the tested concentration of each nanomaterial and the exposure time on the total length [Log (TL)] of zebrafish embryos were conducted using the General Linear Model (GLM) analysis (IBM Corp, 2015). Moreover, GLM analysis was performed in order to examine the effect of each nanomaterial on the behavioral responses of zebrafish embryos. The differences were considered significant at p < 0.001 and marginally significant at p < 0.05.

The UV–vis spectra depicted in Figure S3, illustrate a broad absorption peak at around 340 nm, which can be assigned to the n-π* transition in the oxygen of CO bond or to the transitions of CC and CN bonds due to the formation of excited defect surface states caused by nitrogen. The FT-IR analysis which was carried out to characterize the surface groups of the three CQDs confirmed the presence of NH/OH, CCH, SH, COO– and CS groups (Details are given in the Supporting Information). All these functional groups are significant for the water-solubility of the CQDs and the potential interactions with biological molecules. Aqueous solutions of the CQDs exhibit a faint yellow color under daylight and an intense blue PL under UV light (Figure S4). The emission spectra show maxima at around 430, 450 and 460 nm, when the non-doped CQDs, N-CQDs and N,S-CQDs are excited at 300, 340 and 360 nm, respectively.
The stability of the CQDs was tested for 96 h by monitoring the UV absorption and fluorescence every 24 h; the CQDs were found to be stable.

The TEM images (Fig. 1 .) reveal that all three CQDs have narrow size distribution and quasi-circular shapes with diameters of around 4.0 nm, 2.5 nm and 8.0 nm for the non-doped, N-doped and N,S co-doped CQDs, respectively. Their size is in the low nanometer size, as expected from their fluorescent properties.

The toxic effect is induced in a nanomaterial type- and concentration-dependent manner. The non-doped CQDs are the least toxic of the tested nanomaterials followed by the N-doped CQDs, while N,S-doped CQDs are found to be the most toxic of them. In particular, the lethal doses were estimated to vary among the nanomaterials in a different way, as shown in Fig. 2 . The mortality rate rises as the concentration increases for each of the three CQDs, compared with that of the control group. The lethal doses for the non-doped CQDs are: LD50 = 584.48 μg/mL, LD25 = 341.354 μg/mL and LD75 = 906.93 μg/mL. The respective values for N-doped are: LD50 = 399.95 μg/mL, LD25 = 176.486 μg/mL and LD75 = 905.73 μg/mL. The lethal dose for N,S-doped CQDs are: LD50 = 149.92 μg/mL, LD25 = 63.468 μg/mL and LD75 = 354.047 μg/mL. Evidently, the exposure to various CQDs induces dose-dependent embryonic toxicity.

Morphological endpoints included pericardial and yolk sac edema (i.e., swelling due to fluid retention) and tail malformation (bending). Exposure to the different CQDs resulted in a dose- and material-dependent increase of these morphological endpoints. In the case of the non-doped CQD-exposed group, yolk sac edema was observed at high concentrations (i.e. 650 μg/mL)which appeared on the last day of the experiment (after 96 h). The group exposed to N-doped CQDs showed significant malformations starting 24 h after exposure. Severe yolk sac edema (Fig. 3 E-H) and tail bending (Fig. 3I-L) were observed even at a concentration of 150 μg/mL and showed a tendency to increase as the concentration increased. The same endpoints were observed for the N,S-doped CQD-exposed group, albeit at a much lower concentration of exposure (i.e. at 75 μg/mL). They were severe and tended to be more pronounced at high concentrations (Fig. 3. M–T). These findings can be justified by the LD50, LD25 and LD75 values, already reported.

The locomotor activities of the zebrafish larvae were recorded at 144 hpf in order to determine whether the exposure to CQDs of concern had a persistent effect on larval behavior (Pitt et al., 2018; Ahmad et al., 2012; Tzima et al., 2017; Crosby et al., 2015). This exposure showed a significant effect on the behavioral parameters compared with the control group (Fig. 5 ). In the visible light test, the total swimming distances of larvae increased in ascending order of toxicity of the CQDs, i.e. non-doped < N-doped < N,S-doped (F = 3733,8; P < 0,001). A hyperactivity was induced to zebrafish in the dark cycle compared with the light cycle, regardless of the tested nanomaterial (F = 1575.3; P < 0.001). This effect was nanomaterial-dependent meaning that the nanomaterial with the lowest LD50 value (i.e. the most toxic) induced hyperactivity. It is noteworthy that as the toxicity of CQDs increased the distinction between the light and dark total movement of larvae became more pronounced (F = 1819.6; P < 0.001) (Fig. 5).

Environmental exposure to nanomaterials is inevitable since they have become a part of our daily life. Hence, increasing attention has been focused on nanotoxicity research. The zebrafish is a promising model organism for assessing the toxicity and its millimeter-sized embryos allow us to study the in vivo toxicity and nanomaterial uptake by the entire organism. In this study, the exposure to different CQDs (non-doped, N-doped, N,S-doped) at different concentrations, starting at 24 until 96 hpf period has revealed persistent effects on the viability, morphology and behavior of zebrafish embryos. Our findings demonstrated that the exposure to CQDs led to embryonic developmental toxicity in zebrafish and affected the larval locomotor activity.

According to Yang et al., 2009 and Kang et al., 2015, in vitro experiments in cell cultures showed that CQDs produced low cytotoxicity (Yang et al., 2009; Kang et al., 2015). However, the assessment of an in vivo toxicity of CQDs is indispensable. Zebrafish embryos are more sensitive to external substances at the earlier rather than larval or adult stages. Therefore, in oder to evaluate the potential CQDs toxicities, the embryonic period (24−96 hpf) was selected. In our study, serious malformations were observed in embryos including pericardial edemas. These results are in compliance with other similar studies that dealt with zebrafish exposed to certain nanomaterials (Duan et al., 2013a, b; Zhang et al., 2012). Although CQDs show lower toxicity than metal-based analogs (e.g. CdTe) (Duan et al., 2013a; Zhang et al., 2012), our results reveal that embryos exposed to CQDs exhibit an increasing toxic response, as their doping with nitrogen and sulfur take place and as the concentration within each group increases. Evidently, the CQDs not only provoke toxicity as confirmed by relevant in vitro studies in cell culture (Yang et al., 2009) but also cause various effects to a more complex organism like zebrafish. Importantly enough, the permeability of the organism to these ultra-tiny nanomaterials at its early-life stages can only be feasible through the epithelial tissue, such as gills and epidermis (Bisesi et al., 2014; van Pomeren et al., 2017). Swallowing through the mouth is feasible only after 144 hpf (van Pomeren et al., 2017). In order to investigate the intake of CQDs, the observation through a fluorescence microscope was followed but it did not provide exploitable results. However, CQDs seem to accumulate at 24 hpf to 48 hpf to the gills and epidermis and after 48 hpf they penetrate the internal organs, such as the gastrointestinal tract and liver (Connolly et al., 2016; Kashiwada, 2006). Until now, CQDs have been considered harmless to other model organisms, such as Daphnia and mouse, even at high concentrations (Yang et al., 2009; Osborne, 2013). Our results showed toxicity, with the LD values being notably low for the N-doped and N,S-doped CQDs.

The behavioral study showed that the total distance (mm) covered by larvae was increased when they were treated with N,S-doped CQDs (i.e. the most toxic of them). The difference between the light and dark total movement was constantly increased when the larvae were treated with the most toxic of the studied CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5, the light-dark periods produced consistent patterns of locomotor activity. In visible light, the movements of the larval zebrafish were lower compared with those of the dark periods. During the dark period, the motility of zebrafish larvae increased and the highest values of total distance were recorded after exposure to the most toxic N,S-doped CQDs. In our study, zebrafish behavioral activity was increased in the dark period as a response to CQDs treatment; this was confirmed by other studies (Ahmad et al., 2012; Noyes et al., 2015; Tzima et al., 2017; Bailey et al., 2013). The above findings suggest that the CQDs disturbed the neurobehavior of the larval zebrafish. However, the physiological and biochemical mechanisms of the CQD-induced locomotive behaviors in response to the photostimulation are still unclear. The alteration in the larval locomotor activity induced by CQD treatment might be related to the production of oxidative stress by reactive oxygen species that lead to apoptosis and cell death, as reported in and proved by other studies (Li et al., 2015; Fard et al., 2015). Further studies are necessary to elucidate the potential mechanisms of the CQD-induced biochemical and physiological changes and to explore the stress-related behavioral responses in the larval zebrafish. To summarize, our results, also, indicate that the behavioral analysis of zebrafish is a sensitive tool to detect the sub-lethal effects of chemicals, as mentioned in other studies (Kane et al., 2004). In addition, the larval zebrafish is an emerging and promising high-throughput model organism for neurobehavioral research because of its well-characterized genome, robust behavioral responses and physiological similarity to human (Bailey et al., 2013; Lieschke and Currie, 2007; Riehl et al., 2011).
Data concerning anything from the current project can be given from the corresponding author: Ioannis Leonardos.

Ieremias Chousidis: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Constantine D. Stalikas: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Supervision, Writing - original draft. Ioannis D. Leonardos: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors thank Dr. Chatzimitakos Theodoros and Dr. Kasouni Athanasia for their assistance in the synthesis and preparation of CQDs. The assistance of the undergraduate students Georgakaki Nikoleta, Athanasiou Alexandros and Kouveli Eva with the zebrafish care and maintenance is highly acknowledged. Part of this study was supported by IKY (State Scholarship Foundation) through Doctorate Scholarship with the reference number 14653 (Partnership Agreement 2014-2020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.etap.2020.103426.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
